forecasts.reasoning: Starting from the 0.225 base rate, I adjust upward modestly because Sri Lanka did see several protest-related episodes in 2025 (student/union protests in Colombo, May memorial gatherings, and the sustained Mannar environmental protest), and civic-space monitors describe ongoing protest disruption rather than an absence of mobilization. However, I do not go high because the latest known reference value was still 0.000 as of 2025-10-30, recent searches did not surface clear major protests in late Oct/early Nov 2025, and state repression appears to suppress protest frequency. I also assume the Nov-9 reference average is still likely below 1, so a single ACLED-coded protest would probably be enough for YES. Thus: lower probability for 2025-11-16 because much of that 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date without clear evidence of an event; somewhat higher for 2025-12-09 because the full 30-day window is still ahead; and slightly closer to 0.5 for 2026-02-07 to reflect greater horizon uncertainty while remaining below even odds.

Finland’s ACLED baseline is essentially 1.0 as of 2025-10-30, so a YES likely requires roughly 11+ fatalities in the prior 30 days. That is a very high bar for Finland. Available pre-2025-11-09 evidence shows no sign of any recent large fatal political-violence episode in Finland; Reuters snippets for 2024-25 show incidents with 0-1 fatalities or injuries only (e.g. 2024 school shooting with 1 death, 2025 school attack with injuries, 2025 Tampere stabbing with injuries). Supo’s threat level is elevated and far-right risk has risen, but the cited threat is mainly from lone actors/small groups, which is more consistent with low-casualty attacks than with 11+ fatalities in a 30-day window. Finland’s history does include rare mass-casualty outliers, so the probability is not zero, and it rises with horizon. For 2025-11-16 the window is mostly already known by the cutoff, making a spike especially unlikely; for 2025-12-09 and 2026-02-07 there is more time for a rare tail event, but it still remains unlikely overall.

The key signal is that the ACLED-based reference value was 0.000 as of 2025-10-30, which strongly suggests Saint Vincent and the Grenadines had no recorded ACLED 'Violence against civilians' events in the preceding ~year, despite many ordinary homicides. That implies most local killings are not being coded by ACLED into this category, likely because they lack a clearly identified organized armed actor or do not meet ACLED’s inclusion rules. This pushes the forecast below the generic 0.225 base rate. Offsetting that, 2025 reporting shows elevated gang/gun violence, 28+ homicides by early October, a May triple homicide, and a fresh Nov. 5 shooting, so there is still some chance at least one incident in a given 30-day window is coded as violence against civilians. For 2025-11-16, most of the window was already effectively observed by the forecast date and lacked an obviously qualifying ACLED event, so it is lowest. The later dates get modestly higher mainly because of greater uncertainty and more time for a qualifying gang/state attack to occur, but they remain well below 0.5 because the past-year ACLED signal is still zero.

Kuwait appears to be a very low-violence ACLED case as of 2025-11-09: the known 360-day baseline is 0.000, recent conflict trackers do not flag Kuwait, and available reporting points to repression and occasional protests but not deadly political violence. Since the threshold is effectively zero, any ACLED-coded fatality in the target 30-day window would make this resolve YES, so the probability cannot be negligible. Still, compared with the generic 0.225 base rate, Kuwait-specific evidence supports a substantially lower estimate. I therefore use low probabilities that rise modestly with horizon to reflect increasing uncertainty: 2025-11-16 very low, 2025-12-09 slightly higher, 2026-02-07 higher still but still well below 0.5.

Baseline is currently 1.000, so each date needs 11+ ACLED-coded Battles in the prior 30 days. That is a very high bar for Lithuania, which appears to have had essentially no recent battle activity. Up to the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the evidence shows elevated regional tension—Russian/Belarusian pressure, airspace/drone/balloon incidents, cyber and hybrid activity, drills, and Baltic contingency planning—but not actual sustained armed clashes inside Lithuania. Most of these incidents would not obviously code as ACLED Battles. So a YES outcome likely requires a sudden, major escalation into repeated cross-border or interstate combat on Lithuanian territory, which remains unlikely, especially with Russia still facing costs and constraints from the Ukraine war. I therefore stay at the floor for the nearest date, then move slightly upward for later dates to reflect greater horizon uncertainty and somewhat more time for an escalation scenario to emerge.

French Polynesia appears to have an exceptionally low underlying risk of ACLED-coded Battles. The known reference value was 1.000 as of 2025-10-30, which strongly suggests zero Battles in the prior 360 days, so the effective threshold is about 11+ Battles in a 30-day window. Search evidence indicates no modern pattern of armed conflict in French Polynesia; tension there is mostly electoral/decolonization politics, protests, or public-order issues, not sustained armed-group fighting. Regional unrest in New Caledonia raises tail risk, but even that does not imply an organized armed conflict dynamic in French Polynesia itself. For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date with no visible sign of a large battle spike, so that date gets the lowest probability. I raise the probabilities only slightly for the later dates to reflect greater uncertainty and more time for an unexpected shock, but all remain very low.

Baseline is effectively zero: the latest known 360-day reference average for ACLED ‘Riots’ in Myanmar was 0.000, so this is close to asking whether there will be at least one riot-coded event in each target 30-day window. That matters because Myanmar’s conflict is currently intense but is dominated by battles, remote violence, and violence against civilians, not riot-coded events. Evidence up to 2025-11-09 suggests protests have declined sharply under repression/conscription, and even notable anti-junta gatherings are often coded as protests or attacks on protesters rather than riots. This pushes probabilities below the generic 0.225 base rate. I raise the odds somewhat for later windows because the junta’s Dec 28 election and follow-on phases into January increase the chance of anti-election unrest, but much of that unrest is still more likely to appear as protests, armed clashes, or attacks than ACLED ‘Riots.’ For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window is already in the past as of the forecast date, which lowers the chance further.

The baseline is effectively zero: the latest known reference value on 2025-10-30 was 1.000, implying roughly no recent ACLED 'Violence against civilians' events in the prior 360 days. That means a YES likely requires about 11+ such events in a 30-day window (possibly 12 if the final baseline ticks up slightly), which would be an extreme jump for Albania. Albania does have organized-crime violence and an October 2025 courtroom killing, but those are isolated incidents and do not clearly imply a sudden month with double-digit ACLED VAC events. More importantly, recent general crime reporting has not translated into a nontrivial ACLED VAC baseline. So I stay near the floor for all dates, with only a modest increase for the later windows because there is more time for an unexpected destabilizing shock before those 30-day periods begin.

This looks like a very hard YES. The key point is the reference value was reported as 1.000 as of 2025-10-30, implying ACLED had effectively zero recorded Battles in Jordan over the prior 360 days, so the likely threshold is about 11+ Battles in a 30-day window (possibly 12 if there are small retrospective updates, but still very high). Open-source reporting before 2025-11-09 shows only isolated armed border clashes with smugglers/infiltrators on a few dates (not a dense run of daily clashes), plus Jordan-Syria coordination efforts to suppress smuggling. That is far short of the sustained tempo needed for 11+ ACLED Battles in one month inside Jordan. For 2025-11-16, most of the 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, making a spike especially unlikely. I raise the probability slightly with horizon because regional spillover risk from Syria/Iran-border dynamics could increase uncertainty, but even by 2026-02-07 Jordan would still need an abrupt and unusually intense deterioration to get above the threshold.

Baseline prior is extremely low: the most recent known reference value on 2025-10-30 was 1.000, which implies roughly zero ACLED 'Battles' in Argentina over the prior 360 days. Even if small backfills occur by 2025-11-09, the trigger is still about 11-12 battles in a 30-day window. That would be a very sharp jump from recent history. Argentina does have organized-crime violence centered on Rosario, but 2024-2025 reporting points to a crackdown, much lower homicide totals in 2024 versus 2022-2023, and 2025 reporting that violence persists only in pockets/peripheries rather than broad escalation. Also, much of Rosario violence appears to be killings/threats and may code as violence against civilians or other ACLED categories, not necessarily 'Battles.' For 2025-11-16 the bar is especially hard because the known 1.000 value through 2025-10-30 already covers much of the relevant 30-day window, so the needed spike would have to happen in the remaining days. I therefore put the near-term date at the floor, with only slight increases for later dates because longer horizons allow somewhat more chance of a sudden gang-war or state-gang clash surge.

Baseline is effectively zero, so this is almost entirely a question of whether ACLED will record at least 1 Strategic development in each 30-day window. I start from the 0.225 prior, then adjust for Hungary-specific evidence. The main downward adjustment is that Hungary’s known 360-day reference value was 0.000 as of 2025-10-30, despite several newsworthy political/legal moves in 2025; that suggests ACLED codes Strategic developments in Hungary sparingly. The main upward adjustment is that Hungary had a notable Oct. 21 extension of the state of emergency, which falls inside the Nov. 16 window and is the kind of major security/political measure ACLED sometimes codes. However, similar earlier extensions apparently did not show up in the recent ACLED count, so I do not push the short-horizon probability very high. For the later windows, I keep probabilities only modestly above the prior: the approach of the 2026 election, continued emergency rule, migration-policy disputes, and EU rule-of-law conflict create some chance of a codable strategic event, but Hungary’s recent ACLED history argues against a large increase. I also move the later forecasts slightly closer to 0.5 to reflect greater horizon uncertainty.

The threshold is effectively 11+ ACLED fatalities in a 30-day window, because the reference value near 2025-11-09 is 1.000. That is a very high bar for Martinique. Evidence before the cutoff suggests: (1) Martinique’s major recent unrest was the Sep–Oct 2024 cost-of-living wave; even that appears to have caused only about 1–4 deaths total over roughly six weeks, still far below 11 in 30 days; (2) 2025 reporting focuses on trials, political grievances, water shortages, and lingering anger, but I found no clear evidence of renewed mass-fatality unrest in 2025; (3) Martinique is a small French territory with limited armed-group activity and substantial state policing capacity. So the chance of an 11+ fatality spike is very low on all dates. I raise the probability slightly with horizon because there is more time for unrest to re-emerge or escalate unexpectedly.

Evidence before the cutoff suggests: (1) Martinique’s major recent unrest was the Sep–Oct 2024 cost-of-living wave; even that appears to have caused only about 1–4 deaths total over roughly six weeks, still far below 11 in 30 days; (2) 2025 reporting focuses on trials, political grievances, water shortages, and lingering anger, but I found no clear evidence of renewed mass-fatality unrest in 2025; (3) Martinique is a small French territory with limited armed-group activity and substantial state policing capacity. So the chance of an 11+ fatality spike is very low on all dates. I raise the probability slightly with horizon because there is more time for unrest to re-emerge or escalate unexpectedly.

Starting from the 0.225 base rate, I adjust upward because Indonesia—especially Papua—had persistent lethal political violence through 2024-2025. Pre-cutoff reporting and ACLED-based summaries indicate: 2023-2024 were the most violent years in ACLED’s Indonesia coverage; 85 of 116 national fatalities in Jan-Oct 2024 were in Papua; major 2025 lethal episodes included the April killings of 17+ miners, a May operation with 18 separatists and 3 civilians reportedly killed, at least 10 fatalities from August 2025 civilian-targeting operations, and a mid-October 2025 operation with roughly 14-15 killed. That said, ACLED inclusion/backfill is uncertain, and the platform’s stated reference value as of 2025-10-30 was 0.000, which strongly suggests the comparison threshold may be very low, but not guaranteed to stay there. For 2025-11-16, most of the 30-day window had already elapsed by the knowledge cutoff, and I did not find clear large ACLED-relevant fatal events after the mid-October battle, so I’m above-even but not extreme. For 2025-12-09 and 2026-02-07, ongoing Papua conflict keeps YES more likely than the generic base rate, but uncertainty about future violence and final ACLED coding/backfill pushes the farther-dated forecasts closer to 0.5.

Suriname looks like a very low-ACLED-fatality environment at the 2025-11-09 cutoff. The question itself says the most recent known 360-day reference average (as of 2025-10-30) was 0.000, which strongly suggests the comparison threshold will likely remain zero or very close to zero. For YES, that likely means at least one ACLED-coded fatality in the relevant 30-day window. Evidence I found points to a calm recent context: international observers described the May 2025 election and July 2025 presidential transition as peaceful, with no reported fatalities (search_4_summary). I found no strong evidence of new fatal political violence in late 2025. Suriname does have some tail risk from protest unrest and mining-related clashes, as seen historically in 2023, but ordinary accidents like mine collapses may not be ACLED-coded political fatalities, and there is no sign of an active insurgency or sustained violent campaign. So I go well below the 0.225 generic base rate, but not extremely low because even one isolated riot/clash/security-force incident would be enough if the baseline stays at zero. Shortest horizon is lowest because most of that 30-day window had effectively already passed by the cutoff without obvious signs of fatalities; farther horizons are a bit closer to 0.5 due to greater uncertainty.

Base rate for 10x ACLED spikes is extremely low, and Nicaragua-specific evidence pushes against a near-term riot surge: the latest reference value is 1.000 (so the practical threshold is about 11+ riots in a 30-day window), while 2024-2025 reporting describes a highly consolidated police state, mass raids, expanded coercive capacity, censorship, exile, and suppression of public mobilization rather than active street clashes. The main historical riot wave was 2018, not a current pattern. I therefore put the first date at the floor. I raise the later dates modestly because uncertainty increases with horizon and there is more time for an unforeseen trigger (including regime actions or early election-cycle unrest) to produce a temporary spike, though it still looks unlikely.

North Macedonia appears to have had essentially zero recent ACLED 'Explosions/Remote violence' events; the known reference value on 2025-10-30 was 1.000, which implies a minimum YES threshold of 11 events in a 30-day window, and any late updates would more likely raise than lower that threshold. Searches found no evidence of an active insurgency, bombing campaign, or recurring remote-violence pattern in 2024-2025—only a tragic nightclub fire, protest unrest, bomb hoaxes, and terrorism-related arrests/seizures, none of which imply 11+ qualifying remote-violence events are likely in a month. North Macedonia is a relatively low-conflict state, so reaching 11 events would probably require a sudden major terrorist or cross-border armed escalation. I therefore stay at the floor for the nearest date and increase only slightly with horizon to reflect growing uncertainty.

Baseline is currently 1.000, so a YES would require roughly 10-11+ ACLED 'Battle' events in a 30-day window in the Falkland Islands. That would imply a genuine armed outbreak, not just rhetoric. The evidence up to 2025-11-09 points the other way: no recent battles in 2023-2025, tiny local population, strong UK deterrent presence, and the sovereignty dispute remaining overwhelmingly diplomatic. Milei has continued the claim rhetorically, but reporting also emphasizes peaceful/diplomatic approaches, UN calls for dialogue, and even UK-Argentina military dialogue. So the chance of a sudden cluster of 10+ organized armed clashes is extremely low. I raise the probability slightly with horizon because there is more time for an unexpected crisis or ACLED coding surprise, but it remains at the low end of the allowed range.

Canada’s reference level is effectively zero: the most recent known 360-day baseline average for Battles in Canada was 0.000, implying no ACLED-coded battles in the prior year. So each resolution mostly reduces to: will there be at least one ACLED-coded Battle in that 30-day window? That remains unlikely. Canada has no active insurgency or civil conflict, and reporting before 2025-11-09 points to declining gang violence in B.C. and a disrupted Quebec militia plot rather than ongoing armed clashes. Still, risk is not zero because ACLED could code a gang shootout, biker-gang clash, or rare armed confrontation involving organized groups as a Battle. The 2025-11-16 window is lowest because most of that window had already elapsed by the forecast date with no clear sign of such an event; later dates are nudged slightly upward to reflect greater uncertainty, while remaining well below the generic 0.225 prior base rate.

The reference value is very likely about 1.0, so a YES resolution probably requires roughly 11+ ACLED-coded riots in a 30-day window. I found no evidence by 2025-11-09 of sustained, nationwide mob violence on the scale needed for 11+ riot events in a month. So I stay near the floor for the near date, with only a modest increase for the longer horizons to reflect greater uncertainty.

Primary anchor is the FRED AAA10Y trend analysis: as of 2025-11-07 the spread was 1.16, and the tool’s combined estimate for 2025-11-16 exceeding that threshold is 0.34. I keep the first-date forecast very close to that. The recent move into early November was upward, but 1.16 is already high versus most of the recent sample, and the linear trend projection for Nov. 16 is slightly below the threshold, suggesting some mean reversion risk. The one allowed web search adds modest context: investment-grade corporate spreads were generally described as tight/rich in late 2025, with strong demand tending to compress spreads, though some 2025 outlooks allowed for bumps wider. That points against a strong >50% chance that this elevated early-November level is still exceeded soon. For the later dates, I move probabilities somewhat back toward 0.5 because horizon uncertainty increases and macro/credit conditions could still widen spreads again by year-end or early 2026. Final estimates: 0.35 for 2025-11-16, 0.39 for 2025-12-09, and 0.42 for 2026-02-07.

Primary anchor: the FRED tool’s near-term combined estimate for exceeding the current threshold was 0.49, with the last published value before the cutoff at 4.84 on 2025-11-07. Since 2025-11-09 was a non-month-end Sunday, the comparison level is likely very close to that 4.84 figure. I adjust slightly below the raw 0.49 anchor for the shortest horizon because the linear component of the FRED analysis was weaker (0.31), the series had been in a broader downtrend over the prior months, and contemporaneous macro snippets pointed to lower Treasury yields after resumed Fed cuts. Offsetting that, IG corporate spreads were described as very tight, leaving room for some widening that could push the corporate index yield back up. Net: a bit below even odds in the near term, then drifting closer to 50/50 at longer horizons as uncertainty rises and spread-widening / rate-backup scenarios have more time to occur.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool: with the latest pre-cutoff value at 0.84 on 2025-11-07, its combined estimate for 2025-11-16 exceeding that level is 0.35. I nudge that slightly upward to 0.38 because the last few observations showed some upward momentum and the exact 2025-11-09 comparison value is a bit ambiguous around a non-trading day. Against a large rise: IG corporate spreads were still near historical lows in late 2025, and strong inflows, foreign demand, and post-rate-cut risk appetite were keeping spreads compressed. In favor of some widening over 1-3 months: the index was very tight versus its roughly 1.06% five-year average, so mean reversion and normal credit-risk volatility give more time for a move above the Nov. 9 level. Per instruction, uncertainty increases with horizon, so the later probabilities move closer to 0.5.

FRED’s mechanical short-run anchor (0.71 for Nov 16) is not credible here because this is a policy rate, not a market series: the model is being driven by last year’s higher level and a flat recent line, while actual changes occur only by ECB decision. The question-specific evidence points strongly the other way. As of 2025-11-09, the deposit facility rate was 2.00%, after 200 bps of cuts from 2024 to mid-2025 and then three straight holds. Reuters polling in October 2025 showed 72% of economists expecting no change for the rest of 2025 and 57% expecting no change even through end-2026; market pricing leaned slightly toward cuts by end-2026, not hikes. That makes an increase above 2.00 very unlikely. For 2025-11-16 and 2025-12-09, there was effectively no scheduled opportunity for a hike after the 2025-10-30 hold, so YES would likely require an unscheduled intermeeting move, which is extremely rare. By 2026-02-07 there is more time and at least one or two meetings, so probability rises slightly, but ECB projections and consensus still favor hold/cut over hikes. Hence: 5%, 6%, 9%.

Countervailing factors: this Cleveland Fed series is model-based and monthly, with some input uncertainty from missing CPI data/government shutdown effects; the series is also somewhat noisy, and the current level is near the low end of the past several months, leaving room for a rebound. Those considerations keep the forecast from being too low, especially at the longer horizon.

So I keep the first date close to the tool anchor at 0.32, and for 2026-02-07 I move somewhat back toward 0.5 (more uncertainty with more time for mean reversion/noise), but still below even odds because the prevailing trend/backdrop is downward.

Primary anchor: FRED series SNDR shows a 12-month drift down from 0.43 (2024-11) to 0.40 (2025-11), and the tool’s trend model gives P(value > 0.40 by 2025-12-09) = 0.30. I adjust slightly upward because the last 5 points show mild positive momentum (0.38 in Jun/Jul to 0.40 in Sep-Nov), and this series moves slowly in small increments, so a 1 bp rise is plausible. Still, the broader trend is down / flat, so an increase over the 2025-11-09 level is less likely than not. For 2026-02-07, I keep the probability below 50% because the underlying trend remains soft, but move it somewhat closer to 0.5 to reflect greater horizon uncertainty and more time for a modest rebound. Hence: 0.33 for 2025-12-09 and 0.36 for 2026-02-07.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool’s combined estimate of 0.69 that SOFR would be above the latest known level (~3.93 on 2025-11-07) by 2025-11-16. I adjust that down because the model window straddles a recent Oct. 29 rate cut, and the same-period-2024 comparison likely overstates upside since policy rates were much higher a year earlier. Still, SOFR had stabilized near 3.91–3.93 after the cut, so a modest rebound/increase versus the 2025-11-09 benchmark looks somewhat more likely than not in the very short run. For 2025-12-09, this is still before the Dec. 9–10 FOMC decision, so absent a policy move SOFR should remain near its post-cut range; I therefore keep the probability above 50% but closer to 0.5. For 2026-02-07, at least one and possibly two FOMC meetings intervene. The web evidence shows two 2025 cuts had already occurred, while Powell said a December cut was 'not a foregone conclusion—far from it,' implying meaningful but uncertain odds of further easing by Dec/Jan. That makes SOFR being above the Nov. 9 level by early February slightly less likely than not. I also shade the farthest horizon closer to 0.5 to reflect higher uncertainty and the weekend-date benchmark ambiguity.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool output for WLCFLPCL: last known value before the cutoff was 7,138 (2025-11-05), with a combined near-term estimate of P(exceeding that level on 2025-11-16) = 0.45. The series has clearly shifted up since mid-2025 (from roughly 2-3B to roughly 5-8B), but recent momentum is flat and readings have been oscillating around the current level rather than trending decisively higher. My one web search found evidence of late-October liquidity-facility usage being driven by month-end funding pressures, which suggests some recent strength may be technical/transitory rather than a durable upward move. So I keep the first date near the tool estimate, then move later horizons modestly toward 0.5 because uncertainty rises with time and the threshold is simply the current elevated level in a volatile regime.

By 2025-11-09, the strongest evidence is that BIS rescinded the January 2025 AI Diffusion Rule in May, then issued guidance/policy statements on advanced chips, diversion, and AI-model training risk. Those May actions do raise compliance risk, including for some foreign IaaS/cloud scenarios, but they look more like guidance and D:5/Macau-focused interpretations than a clearly qualifying new, broad global export-control regime. I therefore treat it as uncertain but more likely than not that these steps will NOT be judged sufficient for resolution. The main remaining YES path is a replacement BIS rule or other broad EAR/FR action before 2025-12-31. That is plausible because BIS explicitly said a replacement would come, and AI-chip export controls are a high-priority national-security area with a recent history of frequent revisions. But the delay from May to early November without a visible Federal Register replacement rule is a strong negative signal, and there are less than two months left. Net: meaningful chance, but still more likely NO than YES.

Very low, but not impossible. As of 2025-11-09, no clearly qualifying AI-regulatory bill had passed either chamber; the more substantive proposals (e.g., AI liability / federal framework / preemption bills) were newly introduced and still in committee. Multiple contemporaneous sources described Congress as delayed by the shutdown and other priorities, with federal AI policymaking still incremental rather than comprehensive. The main remaining vehicle is a must-pass year-end bill such as the FY2026 NDAA, which does contain some AI-related provisions, but the strongest candidates appear focused on chips, defense use, research limits, or studies, and may not satisfy this question’s narrow resolution criteria requiring substantive regulation of AI systems or their development. With less than two months left, a qualifying provision could still be added in conference or to another must-pass package, but the path is narrow. Hence I place YES at 5%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, there is no reported Coalition of the Willing deployment into Ukraine. The strongest available reporting says the planned reassurance force, air patrols, and naval/demining elements are intended for after hostilities cease / in the event of a ceasefire, and active fighting was still continuing with Russia rejecting ceasefire proposals into late October. Earlier Reuters reporting also described the effort as shifting away from direct troop deployment because of political and logistical constraints. While planning is advanced and 26 countries have reportedly pledged postwar contributions, the window to get from no ceasefire/no deployment by Nov. 9 to an actual coalition-branded physical presence in Ukraine, Ukrainian airspace, or territorial waters by Dec. 31 is short. I leave a small residual chance for a sudden ceasefire followed by a symbolic or limited coalition air/naval deployment, but overall YES looks very unlikely.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, there are already multiple credible, formal European commitments that appear to satisfy the stated resolution criteria: the UK publicly announced M270 MLRS for Ukraine in June 2022; Norway/UK announced additional M270 transfers in July 2022; France publicly acknowledged additional LRU/M270-type transfers in 2024; and reporting summarized from May 2024 indicates Germany announced it would procure and donate 3 M142 HIMARS launchers to Ukraine. Since the criteria count M142 HIMARS and HIMARS-compatible M270 systems, this looks very likely to resolve YES already. The only meaningful residual uncertainty is possible arbiter interpretation around whether only post-question-opening commitments count, which is not stated in the provided criteria.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the threshold looks very unlikely to be reached in any 2025 quarter. Evidence points to Q1 2025 at only about 11 bcm total Russian gas to the EU (roughly 4.5 bcm pipeline via TurkStream plus ~6.7 bcm LNG), well below 15. In 2024, Russian gas to the EU was about 45 bcm for the full year, with reported quarters around ~11-13 bcm, so 15 bcm would already have been above recent observed levels. After Ukraine transit ended on 2025-01-01, pipeline supply was limited mainly to TurkStream, keeping quarterly pipeline volumes around ~4 bcm rather than high enough to support a 15 bcm quarter. LNG stayed significant, but reports of ~14 bcm Russian LNG to the EU in the first eight months of 2025 imply only ~5 bcm per quarter on average, still leaving total quarterly imports materially below 15 bcm. A YES outcome would likely require an unexpected late-2025 surge in Russian LNG or a major new/renewed pipeline route, neither of which was evident by the cutoff.

Iran has both the capability and recent rhetoric to disrupt Hormuz, and the market’s broad YES criteria mean a selective interdiction of Western shipping could be enough. But the strongest evidence through Nov. 9 points to threats and limited risk actions, not an imminent closure: even during the June 2025 Israel-Iran war, merchant traffic kept moving, though reduced; after the ceasefire, shipping costs fell and the feared full disruption did not happen. August advisories focused on seizure/boarding risk, and October reports framed renewed closure threats mainly as deterrence against sanctions on Iranian oil exports. A real closure or severe restriction would also damage Iran’s own exports, alienate Gulf states and China, and invite heavy U.S./regional military response. So the risk by Dec. 31, 2025 is low, but not tiny because a fresh strike, sanctions enforcement push, or miscalculation could still trigger a short, severe disruption.

Very unlikely. By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, Musk was still Tesla CEO, and the main near-term path to his departure—a failed shareholder vote on his huge compensation package—appears to have been removed by the package’s approval on Nov. 6-7. Tesla’s board has publicly emphasized retaining him, denied earlier replacement-search reports, and the new pay structure itself rewards continued leadership. With only ~7 weeks left in 2025, a CEO change would most likely require an abrupt resignation, board rupture, or external shock. Those tail risks exist, but the base rate is low, so I put this at the floor of the allowed range.

Leclerc appears to hold a substantial late-season points lead over Hamilton, and the available pre-cutoff evidence suggests Brazil did not help Hamilton close it. On Oct. 5, standings showed Leclerc on 173 vs Hamilton on 127 (a 46-point gap). By early Sept. Hamilton was also reported 46 points behind, so this was not a tiny or volatile margin. In the Sao Paulo sprint on Nov. 8, Leclerc finished ahead of Hamilton, slightly widening the gap; then both Ferrari drivers failed to score in the Nov. 9 GP, so Hamilton did not make a comeback there. With only three races and one sprint left after Sao Paulo, Hamilton would need an unusually large late swing against a teammate who has generally been ahead of him on points and pace. It is still mathematically possible, but only via a very favorable combination of Hamilton strong finishes and Leclerc poor results. I put YES at 5%.

By 2025-11-09, the strongest pro-YES evidence is that the US and Iran already had direct hostilities in June 2025: US strikes on Iranian nuclear sites, an Iranian missile response against a US base, and broad media language about a short air war. But that episode ended within days in a ceasefire, and the market’s own clarified resolution standard is materially stricter than 'one round of strikes' or a single headline. The creator said limited targeted strikes are not sufficient; YES requires something more like a sustained campaign, clear public consensus of active war, regime-change intent, or major ongoing conflict. As of late October/early November, the situation appears tense but not on that trajectory: Reuters reporting points to a shaky but real post-June ceasefire, resumed/attempted diplomacy, and later pressure via sanctions/snapback rather than sustained US military operations. Snapback sanctions, Iranian nuclear escalation, and proxy risks keep the tail risk alive, but with less than two months remaining, the bar for resolution is high and the window is short. So YES is possible but unlikely.

Q1 2025 was negative, but Q2 was clearly positive, so this market now requires both Q3 and Q4 2025 to print negative on their initial BEA estimates. As of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the scheduled Q3 advance BEA release had been delayed by the government shutdown, but the main real-time trackers were still solidly positive: Atlanta Fed GDPNow around 3.8-4.0% and NY Fed around 2.3-2.4%. That makes a negative Q3 initial print possible but very unlikely. Q4 does have meaningful downside risk because CBO estimated the shutdown could subtract roughly 1-2 percentage points from Q4 annualized growth, so a negative Q4 print is much more plausible than a negative Q3 print. But for YES, both have to be negative consecutively, and the low probability of Q3 being below zero dominates. My unconstrained estimate is only a few percent, but given the required floor I submit 5%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, I found no NBER Business Cycle Dating Committee announcement identifying a peak in 2023 or 2024, and the official NBER business-cycle pages still showed no post-2020 peak. The macro data for 2024 also look inconsistent with a broad recession call: real GDP grew about 2.5% in 2024, payrolls were still adding roughly 180k jobs/month, unemployment was only around 4.1% in late 2024, and real personal income less transfers was still growing. The main contrary signal was the 2024 Sahm Rule trigger, but contemporaneous analysis from CRS and others argued it was likely a false positive driven by labor-force/immigration dynamics rather than broad contraction. There remains a small chance that late-2024 weakness could later be dated by NBER and announced before end-2025, but with no announcement by early Nov 2025 and only weeks left to the deadline, YES appears very unlikely. Using the allowed floor, I estimate 5%.

Current official WR is 2:00:35 (Kiptum), so a legal sub-2 requires another 36-second improvement on the best-ever record. By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the fastest 2025 legal marks were still around 2:02:16-2:02:23, even at very fast races like Berlin/Chicago. The strongest likely remaining opportunity before year-end is Valencia on 2025-12-07, but the announced field is led by Sisay Lemma (PB 2:01:48) and several 2:04-high runners; it does not look stronger than the opportunities that already failed to produce even 2:01. With Kiptum gone and no obvious late-2025 entrant already sitting on sub-2:01 form, a legal sub-two by Dec. 31, 2025 looks possible but very unlikely. I set it at 5%.

As of 2025-11-09, this had not happened again yet: the April 4 drop was about -6%, but subsequent reporting indicates the worst later one-day decline was only about -2.7% on Oct. 10, described as the biggest drop in six months. By early November the S&P 500 was near record highs (around 6729 on Nov. 7), and volatility was elevated but not crisis-level: Reuters reported the VIX around 19.7 in mid-October, far below the panic levels typically associated with >5% close-to-close crashes. Historically, >5% daily S&P declines are rare and usually cluster in severe crisis regimes (1987, 2008, 2020). The remaining window to year-end was only about 7 weeks / ~35-37 trading days, which further limits the chance. Still, 2025 had unusual policy/tariff shock risk and some political/economic instability, so the chance is not negligible. Net: low, but not tiny — about 7%.

As of the Nov 9, 2025 cutoff, the strongest available evidence points strongly against >80% on FrontierMath by Dec 31, 2025. Epoch AI reported that the best single-run performance seen on FrontierMath was only about 29%, with GPT-5 (medium) around 29% on Tiers 1–3 and Gemini 2.5 Deep Think at 29% on Tiers 1–3 / 10% on Tier 4. Even aggregating across many models and attempts, only 57% of problems had ever been solved at least once, and Epoch explicitly argued that less than 70% of the benchmark is within reach for current models. Pass@N gains for GPT-5 showed strong diminishing returns, suggesting brute-force inference won’t close the gap. With less than two months remaining in 2025, reaching >80% would require an enormous and historically unprecedented jump from the current frontier. I leave a small residual chance for an unexpected late-2025 model release, benchmark/protocol differences, or hidden evaluations, but the threshold is so far above observed performance that YES looks very unlikely.

As of 2025-11-09, the 49ers were still alive and reasonably likely to make the playoffs (one projection had them at 76.1%), helped by a 6-3 record and an easier schedule. But their Super Bowl path looked tough: only a 17.1% division-title chance, likely wild-card/road playoff route, and major injuries had badly reduced their ceiling. Brock Purdy had missed most of the season, while Nick Bosa and Fred Warner were reported out for the year. Midseason betting/projection signals had them around the back half of contenders rather than true favorites (roughly 13th overall / 7th in NFC in one roundup). Their home stadium hosting the Super Bowl is a small plus if they get there, but not enough to offset the injuries and expected playoff seeding. Net: a low single-digit to mid-single-digit chance, and I land at 6%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, BTC was already near a fresh ATH around $126.2k, so the question reduces to whether it can rally roughly another 19% at any point in the remaining ~7.5 weeks. Bullish evidence: very strong 2025 ETF inflows, institutional adoption, supportive Q4 seasonality, rising implied vol into October/November, and some credible bullish targets above $150k. Bearish evidence: several contemporaneous analyst discussions framed $140k as the more likely near-term target, BTC’s volatility in 2025 was lower than in prior cycle blow-offs, and a mature ETF-driven market may limit the odds of a late-2025 vertical spike without a new catalyst. A simple barrier-touch intuition using spot ~126k, barrier 150k, time to year-end, and annualized vol roughly 35-42% points to odds in the low-to-mid 20s; I adjust slightly upward for bullish seasonality and trend momentum. Final estimate: 27%.

NFL owners had approved the ESPN/Disney deal by late October 2025, but as of the Nov. 9 cutoff it still had not closed. Multiple contemporaneous reports said definitive agreements and antitrust clearance were still outstanding; Reuters reported DOJ review was expected and could take up to 12 months, and Sports Business Journal said late 2026 was viewed as the best-case close timeline. Some articles loosely said ESPN 'will take control' of NFL Network/RedZone, but that appears to describe the eventual post-closing structure, not present control. I see little evidence of an interim handover before closing. So YES is possible only if approvals/closing somehow happen unusually fast or if resolution is interpreted loosely; otherwise actual control by end-2025 looks unlikely.

No clear evidence surfaced that SNL had already done a Waymo-related sketch through the Nov. 8, 2025 episode, and transcript search only turned up an older 2021 Waymo joke, not a 2025 one. That means the forecast mostly comes down to the remaining late-2025 episodes. There are only a handful left, which caps the chance. On the other hand, Waymo was quite topical by the cutoff: major expansion news, a federal safety probe over school-bus behavior, and broader robotaxi/media attention (search_3_summary). SNL has also shown willingness to joke about tech/AV news and previously referenced Waymo. Still, SNL is more likely to parody self-driving cars or Elon/Tesla in general than to build a segment specifically around the Waymo brand. Net: a real but modest chance, slightly above 1-in-6.

Chrome looked overwhelmingly likely to remain the most popular browser by end-2025. Multiple pre-cutoff sources citing StatCounter Global Stats put Chrome around 65% global browser share in 2025, versus Safari around 18–19% and Edge around 5% (search_2_summary). Chrome had held a very large lead for years, and the forecast horizon from 2025-11-09 to 2026-01-01 was under two months. For this to resolve NO, either browser usage would need to shift implausibly fast or the Wikipedia table/source methodology would need to change in some unusual way. I leave a small margin below certainty for possible resolution/source quirks, but Chrome was by far the most probable winner.

Musk publicly announced an 'America Party' in July 2025, but the best pre-2025-11-09 reporting consistently said there were no real follow-through steps: no authentic FEC filing, no clear official website/infrastructure, and no tangible launch beyond social-media statements. As late as Aug. 20, CNN and others still described little concrete action, and there was no strong later evidence of a real launch. Given the market’s subjective resolution standard, a late-2025 YES is still possible because Musk could create a party quickly if he chose, but after ~4 months of inactivity following the initial announcement, that looks unlikely. I therefore put the chance modestly above the 5% floor at 6%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the freshest and most direct evidence points to a thaw: Trump said on 2025-10-27 that things were 'good' with Musk, that he likes him, and that they had been speaking again since September; reporting also described Musk as back in Trump’s good graces after their summer feud. That is strong evidence for the current state, and the horizon to 2025-12-31 is fairly short. The main reason not to go higher is volatility: their 2025 relationship swung from close alliance to very public rupture, then partial reconciliation, and both men are impulsive and politically self-interested. Because resolution is subjective and based on end-of-year news tone, another public blowup could still flip this to NO. Netting those factors, I put YES at 79%.

As of the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, there is clear evidence of outrage and rhetorical retaliation from the White House, but no public reporting of an actual Trump legal action specifically against South Park over the parody. Multiple sources discuss only the White House statement and emphasize that satire of a public figure is strongly protected by the First Amendment. Trump did previously extract a $16M settlement from Paramount over 60 Minutes, but that appears tied to a separate dispute and merger-era leverage; by this cutoff, the Paramount-Skydance merger had already been approved/closed, reducing that leverage. For this market to resolve YES, Trump must still initiate some concrete legal/policy action in the remaining ~7 weeks of 2025 and it must produce negative repercussions (restriction, settlement, revenue loss, etc.). Given the absence of such action after months of sustained South Park mockery, the short remaining time window, and weak legal merits, YES looks possible but clearly unlikely.

By 2025-11-09, Iran faced serious stressors—war damage from June, renewed sanctions, recession risk, elite infighting, and uncertainty around Khamenei’s health/succession. But the evidence still points much more toward regime continuity than collapse before 2025 ends: Reuters reported no significant post-war mass protests, activists were wary of mobilizing, the opposition was divided, and the regime was actively hardening its command structure (e.g. a new Defense Council, succession/continuity planning) rather than visibly losing control of Tehran. Since resolution requires the current regime to actually lose control of the central government in Tehran within the remaining ~7 weeks of 2025, the window is short and the base rate is very low. Tail risks remain from a sudden succession crisis, coup, or protest cascade, but overall YES is only about 5%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the technical-recession path is extremely narrow. Q1 2025 was negative (about -0.3% advance / -0.5% in later reporting), but Q2 2025 was clearly positive (+3.0% advance, +3.3% second estimate), so there is no Q1-Q2 recession pair. For Q3 2025, contemporaneous nowcasts and previews were solidly positive: consensus around ~2.0% annualized and Atlanta Fed GDPNow around ~3.8-3.9%, with multiple October sources describing growth as healthy. That means a YES outcome would likely require a very large downward revision turning Q3 from clearly positive to negative and Q4 also negative. Such a combo is possible but rare. Broader economist recession odds over 12 months were much higher, but under this market’s narrow rule (two consecutive negative GDP quarters by end-2025) and given the already-positive Q2, the chance is only around the floor of the allowed range.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the main public facts point strongly toward NO: Disney/ABC shut down 538 in March 2025 as part of broader layoffs, laid off the remaining staff, and there is no clear public reporting through the cutoff of a sale, spinoff, relaunch, or revival attempt. The brand still has some residual value, but Disney has shown little interest in operating it, and Nate Silver no longer controls the brand and had already taken his own core forecasting models elsewhere. With less than two months left in the year, a public acquisition or brand revival is still possible but looks unlikely. I leave a small tail for a quick asset sale, limited revival, or publicly reported use of 538 IP, but the base case is that nothing happens before year-end.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, Manifold itself was still widely described as play-money/Mana only. The strongest positive evidence is a pre-cutoff search snippet from Manifold’s X profile saying the founders were launching MNX_fi, a ‘dedicated exchange for the AI economy’ with ‘real-money’ products, which could plausibly qualify as an affiliated platform. But I found no solid independent confirmation that such a platform was already live for legal users in any specific jurisdiction, nor clear evidence it offered qualifying real-money prediction/betting markets rather than generic crypto perps. With less than two months left in 2025, regulatory/compliance friction and lack of confirmed launch details keep YES below even odds, though not negligible because an affiliated crypto product appeared to be in motion.

As of the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the best hard official signal is BLS/FRED: federal employment was already down 97,000 from its January peak by August. The key update after that is Treasury’s Nov. 3 statement that roughly 150,000 federal employees took the Deferred Resignation Program and would separate after Sept. 30. If most of those workers were still on payroll through September, they should largely hit the FRED/BLS federal-employment count in October or later—bringing the implied decline to roughly 247,000 even before considering ongoing layoffs, attrition, and the hiring freeze. OMB also said substantial layoffs had begun in October. So crossing the >250,000 threshold looks more likely than not. Main uncertainty: the October/November BLS releases were disrupted by the shutdown, and not every reported resignation/layoff will translate cleanly or immediately into the CES/FRED series. That keeps this from being a high-confidence YES.

As of 2025-11-09, Musk had publicly floated/announced the 'America Party' in early July, but the purported early FEC filings were widely reported as fake and denied by Musk himself. Credible reporting through August said he was quietly putting the brakes on the project to refocus on his companies, and I found no later credible evidence of a real FEC filing or an active launch effort. With less than two months left until the 2025-12-31 deadline, the path to YES is still possible because Musk can act quickly and has ample resources, but the lack of any authentic filing after months of attention makes it unlikely. I put it modestly above the 5% market because impulsive late action is still plausible, but still clearly low.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, multiple credible outlets report that suspects had already been physically taken into custody, and crucially that at least some of them were believed to be direct participants in the Oct. 19 Louvre robbery, not merely accessories. Reuters / NPR / Guardian / NYT reporting summarized above indicates: (1) four people were charged and remanded in custody by early November, (2) authorities believed three of those were among the four-member gang that physically carried out the heist, and (3) the case against them included DNA evidence, cell-phone links, and partial admissions by two early suspects. The market only needs one physical robber to have been apprehended before Dec. 25, and these arrests occurred well before then. The main residual uncertainty is the market creator’s extra condition: an arrest should not later be undermined by credible reporting suggesting the person was likely not a culprit. But given the number of suspects in custody and the apparent evidentiary basis, the chance that all direct-robber arrests would later prove insufficient seems low. Hence a very high, but not absolute, YES probability.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, AI use in Hollywood is clearly rising, including hidden/script-doctor-style use, and WGA rules do not forbid writers from using AI assistance. But I found no identified $100M+ theatrical release opening by end-2025 that had been reported as using AI for a significant portion of its script. Reporting emphasized secrecy, legal/copyright ambiguity, and labor stigma, which all reduce the chance of an acknowledged case. Late-2025 expert commentary also suggested fully AI features were still years away. With less than two months left in 2025 and no concrete candidate among the remaining blockbusters, the chance of a qualifying acknowledged blockbuster before year-end looks low but not impossible.

As of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, frontier models had made major progress but still looked short of a consensus 'generally educated human' threshold. Evidence before the cutoff shows GPT-5 and Claude Opus 4.1 matching or exceeding human experts only in roughly half of some evaluated knowledge-work tasks, while broader analyses still placed GPT-5 at only about 57% of an AGI-style capability score. Important bottlenecks remained: reliability/uncertainty handling, hallucinations, memory, robust real-world agency, and broad cognitive versatility across contexts. 

Crucially, this market resolves from a poll, not a technical benchmark. A poll asking whether AGI already exists is likely to be stricter and more skeptical than optimistic lab marketing. The contemporaneous market price around 0.07 is consistent with that skepticism. With less than two months left in 2025, there was some chance of another meaningful capability jump or a persuasive demonstration, but not enough to make a YES poll likely. So I put the chance of the end-2025 poll resolving YES at 9%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the strongest evidence points to distress but not imminent insolvency before 2026. Virgin Galactic reported about $508M in cash/cash equivalents/marketable securities at 2025-06-30, guided Q3 2025 free cash flow around -$100M to -$110M, and had also been able to raise equity through its ATM program (search_1_summary, search_2_summary). Even allowing for another quarter of similar burn, that suggests several hundred million dollars of liquidity entering late 2025, while the main debt maturity appears to be the ~$425M convertible notes due in Feb. 2027, not before the resolution date. The company’s business outlook is poor and long-run survival is questionable, but bankruptcy protection within the remaining ~7 weeks before 2026 would usually require a much sharper liquidity crisis, creditor event, or going-concern collapse than the evidence shows. So I put the chance low but not negligible, mainly reflecting execution failure, capital-market closure, or an unexpectedly severe financing shock.

As of 2025-11-09, there is still no public confirmation by either Beijing or the U.S. intelligence community that China is operating a military base in any BRICS country. The strongest BRICS-linked candidate is the UAE: U.S. officials previously flagged suspicious construction at Khalifa Port, but reporting through 2025 still describes this as a possible or halted basing effort, not an operating base. U.S. government summaries say China has ‘likely considered’ the UAE for future overseas facilities, which is notably weaker than saying one exists. Iran and Egypt show growing military/security cooperation with China, but the evidence found is about exercises, logistics, and dual-use commercial infrastructure rather than a publicly announced Chinese base. With less than two months remaining until 2026, and no sign of an imminent official announcement, the remaining path to YES looks narrow. I keep some probability because the resolution can be triggered by a U.S. intelligence public announcement rather than Chinese admission, and hidden or dual-use facilities in the UAE remain a live concern. Still, the short time horizon and absence of concrete public confirmation make NO highly likely.

As of 2025-11-09, Chiles' appeal to the Swiss Federal Tribunal is still reported as pending, with no clear sign of an imminent ruling before 2026. The Swiss court can review CAS awards only on very narrow procedural grounds, not on the underlying factual dispute about whether the inquiry was timely. Historical success rates for appeals against CAS awards are extremely low, though Chiles' case does include non-frivolous procedural/conflict arguments. Even so, the combination of (1) a favorable ruling and (2) that ruling arriving before 2026 appears quite unlikely. My unconstrained estimate would be below 5%, but I submit 5% due to the platform's probability floor.

As of Nov. 9, 2025, all publicly reported CDC IRAT assessments for H5 viruses remain in the moderate range, not close to the high threshold of >=8.0. The latest known H5 emergence scores were around 5.1 to 5.8, and May 2025 reporting said newly added US H5N1 viruses were again similar to earlier assessments and still moderate. Broader evidence through Sep 2025 still indicated no sustained human-to-human transmission, low seroprevalence even among exposed workers, and that key adaptations such as stable human-type receptor binding had not yet been established. Just as important, the time window remaining is very short: for YES, CDC would need to publish a new IRAT assessment before Jan. 1, 2026 and that assessment would need to jump by more than 2 points into the high category. Given the combination of unchanged CDC ratings, lack of decisive epidemiologic change, and limited time for a new CDC reassessment, YES looks very unlikely. I would place it below 5% if allowed, but use 5% because of the platform constraint.

As of 2025-11-09, the outcome looks overwhelmingly likely to be YES. The current worldwide box-office leader is Ne Zha 2 at roughly $2.1B-$2.2B, and it is clearly a sequel, hence part of a franchise (search_0_summary, search_1/search_4 snippets). The only remotely plausible film that could overtake it is Avatar: Fire and Ash, but that is also unquestionably a franchise film. Meanwhile, the top non-franchise/original hits are far behind: F1 was around $510.8M and Sinners $365.8M, nowhere near contention (search_2_summary). So virtually every realistic path leads to a franchise movie finishing #1 worldwide for 2025. I leave a small residual margin for classification/reporting ambiguity, but substantively this is very close to certain.

Para el 2025-11-09 ya existía evidencia oficial muy fuerte de que GTA VI no saldría en 2025: Rockstar anunció el 2025-05-02 que el juego pasaba a lanzarse el 26 de mayo de 2026, y medios financieros y del sector confirmaron que Take-Two movió el título a su ejercicio fiscal 2027 (search_0_summary). Dado que la pregunta exige que esté disponible para la venta en Europa dentro de 2025 por canales estándar y autorizados, un lanzamiento oficial en mayo de 2026 implica que la respuesta es abrumadoramente NO. Dejo un 5% en vez de 0% solo por la regla de no usar extremos y por una remota posibilidad de cambio extraordinario o interpretación inesperada, pero en la práctica el resultado parecía casi decidido.

As of 2025-11-09, the available pre-resolution evidence strongly favors YES. Reuters reported Spain’s Q3 2025 real GDP at +2.8% year-on-year based on INE preliminary data, while Eurostat’s flash estimate put euro area Q3 2025 real GDP at +1.3% year-on-year. That is a very large 1.5 percentage point lead for Spain. Since the question resolves on whether Spain’s Q3 2025 y/y growth exceeds the euro area aggregate, ordinary data revisions would have to be extraordinarily large to erase that gap. The main residual uncertainty is technical: final Eurostat publication/revisions and possible small harmonization differences between national and euro-area releases. But given the size of the current lead, reversal looks very unlikely. Hence I assign the maximum allowed high confidence short of certainty.

As of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the key enacted law is the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025. Multiple post-enactment legal/tax summaries describe its §45Y domestic-content changes as changes to the domestic-content bonus/adder thresholds (and related direct-pay consequences), not as making domestic content a prerequisite for the base §45Y production tax credit itself. I found no evidence that any later enacted federal bill had converted the existing domestic-content bonus rules into a mandatory condition for claiming §45Y. With less than two months left in 2025, a second enacted bill making this fairly specific tax change is possible via a year-end package or technical corrections act, but it looks quite unlikely. Hence a low probability, but not zero.

There are strong merits signals for cert: a real and important FIFRA-preemption split (3d Cir. vs. 9th/11th), huge practical stakes from Roundup litigation, and the Court’s June 30, 2025 CVSG, which shows serious interest. Those factors make this much more likely than an ordinary cert petition. But the question is specifically about a grant before Jan. 15, 2026. As of the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the SG brief does not appear to have been filed yet, and CVSG cases often take months; if the SG brief slips into 2026, this resolves No even if cert is later granted. The prior 2022 Hardeman denial is a mild negative, though conditions are more favorable now because the split is clearer and the administration has changed. Net: meaningful chance of a grant, but the calendar is tight enough that I put it below even odds.

As of 2025-11-09, the odds are still low but materially above the earlier ~5% market baseline. Key evidence: (1) Russia’s Novaya Zemlya test site is reportedly maintained and could support testing on short notice; (2) satellite imagery in Aug. 2025 showed unusual activity at Pankovo/Novaya Zemlya; and most importantly (3) on Nov. 5 Putin ordered proposals for possible resumption of nuclear testing, with Defense Minister Belousov urging immediate preparations (search_0_summary, search_1_summary, search_3_summary). That is a real late-breaking escalation signal. 

But I still keep the probability well below 50% because: Russia has not conducted a full nuclear explosive test since 1990/91; an actual test would be globally detectable and politically costly; expert discussion suggests even a simple political demonstration test could take up to months, while only about 7 weeks remained as of the cutoff; and some of the observed Novaya Zemlya activity is also consistent with Burevestnik missile work rather than an actual nuclear detonation. Overall: credible preparation and intent signaling, but short timeline and major deterrents make a 2025 test more unlikely than likely.

Bitcoin had reached about $126k at its October 2025 peak but was trading nearer $113k-$115k by early November. To resolve YES, BTCUSDT on Binance would need a 1-minute high at $170k+, which is roughly a 49% rally from the current level and about 35% above the prior ATH in under two months. Contemporary reports pointed to resistance around $115k-$117k and upside targets more commonly in the $140k-$150k range; options commentary emphasized low implied volatility and increased downside hedging, despite some speculative bets on extreme upside. Strong seasonality, ETF/institutional demand, and bitcoin’s fat-tailed upside keep the event live, but it still looks like a low-probability tail outcome. I estimate 6%.

As of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, Hamas had not made any qualifying official disarmament announcement. The strongest direct evidence is negative: a senior Hamas official told Reuters on Oct. 17/19 that Hamas could not commit to disarm and intended to maintain security control in Gaza. Disarmament was still a major unresolved issue beyond phase one of the ceasefire plan. There are some positive signs: Hamas had accepted phase one of a broader framework that ultimately envisions disarmament, experts reported some private openness to partial steps, and on Nov. 5 Mousa Abu Marzouk floated discussing decommissioning long-range weapons. But that still falls short of a formal policy announcement, and he also said phase-two negotiations had not yet begun. Given the short remaining time to Dec. 31 and the high bar in the resolution criteria for an official announcement, I put the chance at about one in four.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the Fed had made only two 25 bp cuts in 2025: one in September (to 4.00%-4.25%) and one in October (to 3.75%-4.00%), while earlier 2025 meetings were reported as holds (Reuters/CNBC summaries). Only the Dec. 9-10 meeting remained. To reach 8+ cuts for the year, the Fed would therefore need at least 6 more 25 bp cuts at that single remaining meeting, i.e. an extraordinary 150 bp or larger December cut. Contemporary reporting instead showed markets pricing roughly ordinary-sized easing, around a 25 bp cut, and Fed officials were divided even on that. So the true probability is effectively near zero; I submit 5% because the instructions require probabilities between 0.05 and 0.95.

My estimate is 10%. As of 2025-11-09, the dominant baseline was continued easing in 25 bp increments, not a 50 bp move at the January 27-28, 2026 meeting. Reuters polling in late October showed economists expecting 25 bp cuts in late October and again in December, while Goldman and other analysts also described 2026 cuts mainly as two or more 25 bp steps, not a single larger cut (search_0_summary). Market-based commentary around late October likewise suggested more cuts in 2026, but with substantial uncertainty and no strong case for a 50 bp January move specifically; larger-than-25 bp cuts were treated as tail risks (search_1_summary). The labor market was clearly softening, with weak payroll growth and higher jobless claims, which creates some chance of a sharper January response, but inflation was still above target (headline PCE about 2.7%, core PCE about 3.1%, services inflation above 3%), which restrains the Fed from aggressive easing absent a clearer downturn or shock (search_3_summary). So: meaningful downside-risk tail -> nontrivial YES chance, but standard Fed behavior, sticky inflation, and consensus toward quarter-point moves keep NO as the clear favorite.

As of 2025-11-09, Bournazel looks like a clear underdog. The strongest available polling has him only around 14-15% in the first round, well behind Rachida Dati (~26-28%) and Emmanuel Grégoire (~20-22%). The new Paris voting system gives a majority premium to the winning list, which makes his current gap especially important. The main evidence suggests his candidacy hurts Dati more than it creates a path for his own victory: in the cited three-way second-round scenario, the left wins and Bournazel still does not. His upside is that he now has Horizons plus official Renaissance backing, and six months is enough time for some consolidation or a Dati stumble. But from third place at mid-teens to outright winner is still a low-probability path. Net: about a 10% chance.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the evidence leaned slightly against 'refrain' (i.e. slightly toward an announcement by Dec. 31). Reuters and other reporting showed a formal search well underway: Trump had repeatedly said he had a short list, Bessent had narrowed finalists to five, and Bessent said he planned a final round after Thanksgiving and to present Trump a slate in December. Trump also said a decision 'might be made by year-end,' and had earlier suggested an earlier-than-usual timeline/'shadow chair' approach. But the signal was not decisive: the language was hedged, the final interviews were only slated for December, historical Fed chair picks are often closer to the vacancy, and there were process complications tied to early-2026 board vacancies and confirmation timing. Net: announcement by year-end looked a bit more likely than not, so probability Trump refrains from announcing in 2025 is about 41%.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, The Life of a Showgirl had debuted at No. 1 and reached 4 straight weeks atop the Billboard 200. But getting to 18 consecutive weeks would require holding No. 1 for 14 more weeks, into mid-February 2026. That is extraordinarily rare: search evidence indicates the all-time record for consecutive weeks at No. 1 from debut is 13, Taylor Swift’s TTPD reached 12 straight weeks from release and 17 total nonconsecutive, and even other blockbuster recent albums fell well short of 18 straight. Showgirl’s weekly units were already falling sharply after its huge debut, and long streaks generally require both sustained demand and unusually weak competition. Given the historical rarity bordering on unprecedentedness, I put this near the floor at 5%.

As of 2025-11-09, the best read is that a hold at the March 17-18, 2026 FOMC meeting is somewhat more likely than not. Evidence before cutoff points to: (1) strong expectations for additional easing in late 2025, especially an October and likely December cut; (2) for 2026, much higher uncertainty, with Reuters describing the path as very unclear; (3) the Fed’s own September 2025 dot plot showing only one 25 bp cut for all of 2026, which leans toward caution and implies any 2026 move need not come as early as March; (4) market pricing was more dovish than the Fed, roughly 2-3 cuts in 2026, but that still does not make a March move more likely than a hold at that specific meeting. Sticky inflation/tariff concerns raise the bar for early-2026 cuts, while labor-market softening keeps cuts live. Netting these, I estimate roughly a 35-40% chance of a March rate change and about a 63% chance of no change.

As of 2025-11-09, NATO had not invoked Article 5. The strongest evidence is that several serious 2025 incidents that might have been candidates for escalation instead produced Article 4 consultations and deterrence measures: Poland’s Sept. 10 Russian drone incursion and Estonia’s Sept. 19 airspace violation were both handled under Article 4, and NATO launched ‘Eastern Sentry’ rather than moving toward collective-defense invocation. NATO’s own materials emphasize that hybrid attacks could in principle lead to Article 5, but the threshold is politically high and Article 5 has been invoked only once in alliance history (after 9/11). For YES from here, the remaining ~7 weeks of 2025 would likely need a major direct armed attack on a member, or an unusually clear-attributed and severe hybrid event that the North Atlantic Council unanimously treats as equivalent. That is possible given elevated Russia-NATO tensions and expert warnings about limited-attack risk, but still unlikely because deterrence remains strong and recent precedents show alliance caution. Net: low but nonzero residual risk, about 5%.

OpenAI had not publicly announced AGI by the 2025-11-09 cutoff, and the best available reporting still treated AGI as prospective. Sam Altman did make notably bullish statements in 2025 — e.g. that OpenAI is 'confident we know how to build AGI' and that it 'could be getting to AGI this year' — which keeps a nontrivial tail risk alive. But several pieces of evidence point against a declaration by year-end: OpenAI’s own official materials still described AGI as a future goal; major Oct. 2025 Microsoft/OpenAI deal coverage discussed what happens 'if and when' AGI is declared rather than reporting any declaration; and an AGI announcement would have major contractual, governance, and reputational consequences, making OpenAI likely cautious. Given only ~7 weeks remained after the cutoff, I estimate a low but real chance of a late-2025 declaration.

By 2025-11-09, there is no sign that WHO has declared any new coronavirus pandemic other than SARS-CoV-2. The main relevant coronavirus signals are weak: (1) the newly reported bat virus HKU5-CoV-2 had no evidence of human circulation or sustained human-to-human transmission, and experts explicitly said the risk should not be exaggerated; and (2) MERS-CoV had only small numbers of cases in Saudi Arabia in 2025, with WHO maintaining a moderate regional/global risk assessment and saying the new cases did not change its overall view (search_1_summary, search_2_summary). Given WHO’s high bar for using the word “pandemic,” the absence of a fast-growing multinational coronavirus outbreak by Nov. 9, and the short remaining window until Dec. 31, a YES resolution looks very unlikely. I set this at the floor 0.05 rather than lower only because genuinely novel outbreaks can accelerate quickly and WHO wording can be somewhat discretionary.

October 2025 had effectively already occurred by the cutoff, so the main uncertainty is cross-dataset translation to NASA GISTEMP rather than weather itself. The strongest evidence is that Copernicus/ERA5 reported October 2025 as the third-warmest October on record and 2025 overall as on track to be among the three warmest years. In NASA GISTEMP, recent October values were already high (e.g. 1.33C in 2023; around 0.9-1.01C in 2019-2022), and a third-warmest October is more likely than not to land above 1.10C. Berkeley Earth and other 2025 reporting also indicate 2025 remained extremely warm despite some cooling from 2024 peaks. I am not at 2% because the market resolves on a specific NASA monthly table and we do not have the exact NASA Oct 2025 entry before cutoff; cross-dataset/month-to-month differences could in principle put it just under 1.10C. But given the observed warmth, YES (<1.10C) looks unlikely.

Multiple independent reports published before the 2025-11-09 cutoff state that on September 10, 2025 Larry Ellison surpassed Elon Musk on the Bloomberg Billionaires Index, with figures around Ellison $393B vs Musk $385B at about 10 a.m. ET (search_1_summary). The market resolves YES if Musk is any rank other than #1 at any point from June 30 through Dec. 31, 2025, so a brief loss of the top spot would be sufficient. Since the qualifying event appears to have already happened, YES is very likely. I stop short of certainty only because I could not directly inspect Bloomberg’s historical page within the tool constraints, leaving a small residual risk of reporting/interpretation error.

Moonshot looks like a long shot as of 2025-11-09. The strongest direct evidence on the relevant source is that Kimi K2 preview models were only around #11-12 on Chatbot Arena, while Moonshot’s new Kimi K2 Thinking had just launched on Nov. 6 and was not yet listed there. External benchmarks suggest K2 Thinking may be much stronger than prior Kimi models, so a late surge is possible. But for YES, Moonshot would need the new model to be added quickly, accumulate enough Arena votes, actually beat current leaders from OpenAI/Google/Anthropic/xAI, and still be on top on Dec. 31 despite likely further releases. That combination is plausible but clearly low probability. I put it at 10%.

October 2025 was reported by Copernicus/ERA5 (published Nov. 6, 2025) as the third-warmest October on record, and 2025 overall was tracking as a top-3 warmest year. NASA GISTEMP and ERA5 are not identical, but they are highly correlated month-to-month. A third-warmest October in ERA5 strongly suggests NASA’s October 2025 anomaly will land in a relatively high bracket, probably above 1.14°C rather than in the narrow 1.10–1.14°C band. The band is only 0.05°C wide, and the pre-cutoff market price near 0.01 is directionally consistent with it being a long shot. I leave a small floor probability for dataset/baseline translation uncertainty and release-timing uncertainty, but overall this looks very unlikely.

Joe Biden looks like a long shot to finish exactly #2 on Google’s 2025 global Year in Search People list. The strongest evidence against: (1) Google ranks 'trending' people by spike vs prior year, not raw search volume; Biden already had a very high baseline in 2024, which makes a top-2 trend harder. (2) In 2024, despite a huge election year and Biden’s dropout, he still was not in the top 3 global trending people. (3) By Nov. 9, 2025, the more plausible 2025 contenders appear to be figures with sharper novelty spikes such as Pope Leo XIV and Bianca Censori, with Trump also a major candidate. Biden did have meaningful 2025 search catalysts—leaving office, cancer diagnosis, and the 'Original Sin' book—but those seem more consistent with being somewhere in the broader top 10 than landing exactly #2 globally. Given the exact-rank requirement and strong competition, I put this at the floor-low 5%.

Crystal Palace’s chance looked very low as of 2025-11-09. Evidence before the cutoff showed: (1) preseason outright odds around 500/1, later shortened to roughly 66/1 after a strong run, still implying only a slim chance; (2) by early November, Arsenal were clear title favorites at about -225, with most analysis treating the race as Arsenal/Manchester City/Liverpool; (3) projection pieces had Palace finishing around 9th on ~57 points, far off the title pace; and (4) Palace were balancing European commitments, which several analysts expected to hurt league performance. Palace were not mathematically eliminated and had shown some strong underlying numbers, so the chance is not zero, but it is very small. I estimate 5%.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, BTC had been trading roughly in the $100k-$125k range and had recently made new highs above $125k, so hitting $50k by Dec. 31 would require about a 55-60% drop from prevailing levels. That is not impossible for bitcoin historically, but it would be an extreme move over ~7 weeks, especially in a cycle where reported corrections had been much smaller (largest around the mid-20%s on a closing basis). Bullish/institutional support and ETF demand also argue against such a collapse as the base case. Against that, October 2025 showed very high volatility and record liquidations, so tail risk is real. Also, this market resolves on any Binance 1-minute low, so rare exchange-specific flash-crash/scamwick events materially increase YES odds versus a normal end-of-day price target. Balancing a low probability of a genuine crash with a small but nontrivial chance of a Binance wick, I estimate 6%.

Mistral had a meaningful breakthrough in 2025—Mistral Medium 3.1 was reported as #1 in English and #2 overall in a no-style-control LM Arena view by late August. But by the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the top of the leaderboard appears to be led instead by Google/Anthropic/OpenAI/xAI models, with Gemini 2.5 Pro specifically cited as the leader in at least some overall views and Mistral not reported as current #1. The remaining horizon to Dec. 31 is short, so Mistral would likely need a new release or a sharp leaderboard shift to overtake several stronger incumbents. Mistral’s resource base and recent public evidence make that possible but unlikely. Tie-breaks are also unfavorable against some major rivals (e.g. Google, Anthropic). Net: small but nontrivial chance, about 7%.

As of 2025-11-09, Racing Bulls had improved their position with a double-points finish at Sao Paulo, but the available reporting still indicates Williams were 5th and roughly 29 points ahead after Brazil (they were 39 clear before Sao Paulo, then Racing Bulls gained about 10 with Williams scoreless). There were only three races plus one sprint left, so a comeback was still mathematically possible but required a very large swing over a short horizon. Official/team-language also framed Racing Bulls as fighting mainly for P6, not P5, and the midfield behind Williams was tight enough that Racing Bulls also had to avoid being repassed by Aston Martin/Haas/Sauber. The market’s 0.04 on 2025-10-30 seems slightly low after Sao Paulo, but only slightly. Alphabetical tie-break versus Williams helps at the margin if they end level on points. Net: low but non-negligible chance, about 6%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the event had already been played (Aug. 1–3) and multiple pre-cutoff sources indicated results existed and that the winner had effectively leaked before broadcast.
